subject: Chemistry
q:
Compare the energies of following sets of quantum numbers for multielectron system.
(A) \(\mathrm{n}=4,1=1\)
(B) \(\mathrm{n}=4, \mathrm{l}=2\)
(C) \(\mathrm{n}=3,1=1\)
(D) \(\mathrm{n}=3,1=2\)
(E) \(\mathrm{n}=4,1=0\)
Choose the correct answer from the options given below :
question:
Compare the energies of following sets of quantum numbers for multielectron system.
(A) \(\mathrm{n}=4,1=1\)
(B) \(\mathrm{n}=4, \mathrm{l}=2\)
(C) \(\mathrm{n}=3,1=1\)
(D) \(\mathrm{n}=3,1=2\)
(E) \(\mathrm{n}=4,1=0\)
Choose the correct answer from the options given below :
solution:
Energy level can be determined by comparing ( $\mathrm{n}+\ell$ ) values (A) $\mathrm{n}=4, \ell=1 \Rightarrow(\mathrm{n}+\ell)=5$ (B) $\mathrm{n}=4, \ell=2 \Rightarrow(\mathrm{n}+\ell)=6$ (C) $\mathrm{n}=3, \ell=1 \Rightarrow(\mathrm{n}+\ell)=4$ (D) $\mathrm{n}=3, \ell=2 \Rightarrow(\mathrm{n}+\ell)=5$ (E) $\mathrm{n}=4, \ell=0 \Rightarrow(\mathrm{n}+\ell)=4$
For same value of $(n+\ell)$, orbital having higher value of $n$, will have more energy. (B) $>(\mathrm{A})>(\mathrm{D})>(\mathrm{E})>(\mathrm{C})$

q: If the principal quantum number <math> <mrow> <mtext>n</mtext><mo>=</mo><mn>6</mn><mo>,</mo></mrow> </math> the correct sequence of filling of electrons will be:
question: If the principal quantum number <math> <mrow> <mtext>n</mtext><mo>=</mo><mn>6</mn><mo>,</mo></mrow> </math> the correct sequence of filling of electrons will be:
options: <math><mi mathvariant="normal">n</mi><mi mathvariant="normal">s</mi><mo>→</mo><mfenced separators="|"><mrow><mi mathvariant="normal">n</mi><mo>-</mo><mn>1</mn></mrow></mfenced><mi mathvariant="normal"> </mi><mi mathvariant="normal">d</mi><mo>→</mo><mfenced separators="|"><mrow><mi mathvariant="normal">n</mi><mo>-</mo><mn>2</mn></mrow></mfenced><mi mathvariant="normal"> </mi><mi mathvariant="normal">f</mi><mo>→</mo><mi mathvariant="normal">n</mi><mi mathvariant="normal">p</mi></math>

<math><mi mathvariant="normal">n</mi><mi mathvariant="normal">s</mi><mo>→</mo><mi mathvariant="normal">n</mi><mi mathvariant="normal">p</mi><mo>→</mo><mfenced separators="|"><mrow><mi mathvariant="normal">n</mi><mo>-</mo><mn>1</mn></mrow></mfenced><mi mathvariant="normal">d</mi><mo>→</mo><mfenced separators="|"><mrow><mi mathvariant="normal">n</mi><mo>-</mo><mn>2</mn></mrow></mfenced><mi mathvariant="normal">f</mi></math>

<math><mi mathvariant="normal">n</mi><mi mathvariant="normal">s</mi><mo>→</mo><mfenced separators="|"><mrow><mi mathvariant="normal">n</mi><mo>-</mo><mn>2</mn></mrow></mfenced><mi mathvariant="normal">f</mi><mo>→</mo><mi mathvariant="normal">n</mi><mi mathvariant="normal">p</mi><mo>→</mo><mfenced separators="|"><mrow><mi mathvariant="normal">n</mi><mo>-</mo><mn>1</mn></mrow></mfenced><mi mathvariant="normal">d</mi></math>

<math><mi mathvariant="normal">n</mi><mi mathvariant="normal">s</mi><mo>→</mo><mfenced separators="|"><mrow><mi mathvariant="normal">n</mi><mo>-</mo><mn>2</mn></mrow></mfenced><mi mathvariant="normal">f</mi><mo>→</mo><mfenced separators="|"><mrow><mi mathvariant="normal">n</mi><mo>-</mo><mn>1</mn></mrow></mfenced><mi mathvariant="normal">d</mi><mo>→</mo><mi mathvariant="normal">n</mi><mi mathvariant="normal">p</mi></math>
solution:
As per n+l rule electrons fill first in that orbital which have least n+l value.
When n+l values are same, then electron fills that orbital which have lowest n value.
when n=6 as per n+l rule when n=6
ns subshell ⇒6+0=6

n−1 d subshell ⇒5+2=7

n−2 f subshell ⇒4+3=7

np subshell ⇒6+1=7

ns , n-2f, n-1d,np

n+l values ⇒7, 7, 7 

n value ⇒4, 5, 6

λ=h2mqV

λLiλp=18.3×3=15=0.2=2×10-1

subject: Chemistry
q:
Given below are two statements :
Statement (I) : It is impossible to specify simultaneously with arbitrary precision, both the linear momentum and the position of a particle.
Statement (II) : If the uncertainty in the measurement of position and uncertainty in measurement of momentum are equal for an electron, then the uncertainty in the measurement of velocity is $\geqslant \sqrt{\frac{\mathrm{h}}{\pi}} \times \frac{1}{2 \mathrm{~m}}$.
In the light of the above statements, choose the correct answer from the options given below :
question:
Given below are two statements :
Statement (I) : It is impossible to specify simultaneously with arbitrary precision, both the linear momentum and the position of a particle.
Statement (II) : If the uncertainty in the measurement of position and uncertainty in measurement of momentum are equal for an electron, then the uncertainty in the measurement of velocity is $\geqslant \sqrt{\frac{\mathrm{h}}{\pi}} \times \frac{1}{2 \mathrm{~m}}$.
In the light of the above statements, choose the correct answer from the options given below :
options: Statement I is false but Statement II is true
Both Statement I and Statement II are false
Both Statement I and Statement II are true
Statement I is true but Statement II is false
solution:
According to Heisenberg's uncertainty principle, it is impossible to determine simultaneously the exact position and momentum of particle like electron
If 
$\begin{aligned} & \Delta p=\Delta x \\ & \Delta p \cdot \Delta x \geq \frac{h}{4 \pi} \\ &(\Delta p)^2 \geq \frac{h}{4 \pi} \\ & \Delta p \geq \sqrt{\frac{h}{\pi}} \times \frac{1}{2} \\ & m \Delta v \geq \sqrt{\frac{h}{\pi}} \times \frac{1}{2} \\ & \Delta v \geq \sqrt{\frac{h}{\pi}} \times \frac{1}{2 m}\end{aligned}$

subject: Chemistry
q:
The number of atomic orbitals from the following having 5 radial nodes is
7s, 7p, 6s, 8p, 8d
question:
The number of atomic orbitals from the following having 5 radial nodes is
7s, 7p, 6s, 8p, 8d
solution:
The number of radial nodes for an orbital with principal quantum number n and azimuthal quantum number l is given by the expression:

Number of radial nodes = n - l - 1

Using this expression, we can determine the number of radial nodes for each of the given atomic orbitals:

7s:n=7,l=0  7-0-1=6
7p:n=7,l=1  7-1-1=5
6s:n=6,l=0  6-0-1=5
8p:n=8,l=1  8-1-1=6
8d:n=8,l=2  8-2-1=5

Therefore, the atomic orbitals with 5 radial nodes are 7p, 6s, and 8d.

subject: Chemistry
q: The de Broglie wavelength of a car of mass 1000 $\mathrm{kg}$ and velocity $36 \mathrm{~km} / \mathrm{hr}$ is :
question: The de Broglie wavelength of a car of mass 1000 $\mathrm{kg}$ and velocity $36 \mathrm{~km} / \mathrm{hr}$ is :
solution:
$$
\begin{aligned}
& \text { } \lambda=\frac{h}{m v} \\
& h=6.6 \times 10^{-34} \mathrm{~J}-\mathrm{s} \\
& m=1000 \mathrm{~kg} \\
& v=36 \mathrm{~km} / \mathrm{hr}=\frac{36 \times 10^3}{60 \times 60} \mathrm{~m} / \mathrm{sec}=10 \mathrm{~m} / \mathrm{sec} \\
& \therefore \quad \lambda=\frac{6.6 \times 10^{-34}}{10^3 \times 10}=6.6 \times 10^{-38} \mathrm{~m} \\
&
\end{aligned}
$$

subject: Chemistry
q: According to the wave-particle duality of matter by de-Broglie, which of the following graph plot presents most appropriate relationship between wavelength of electron <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mfenced><mi mathvariant="normal">λ</mi></mfenced></math> and momentum of electron <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mfenced><mi mathvariant="normal">p</mi></mfenced><mo>?</mo></math>
question: According to the wave-particle duality of matter by de-Broglie, which of the following graph plot presents most appropriate relationship between wavelength of electron <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mfenced><mi mathvariant="normal">λ</mi></mfenced></math> and momentum of electron <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mfenced><mi mathvariant="normal">p</mi></mfenced><mo>?</mo></math>
solution:
According to the de-Broglie hypothesis according to which all matters have both particle and wave nature. where h= plank's constant, p=momentum, λ= wavelength of particle. This type of graph is called a rectangular hyperbola. As λ∝1p we will have a rectangular hyperbola.

λ=hpλ∝1p

⇒λp=h (constant)

So, the plot is a rectangular hyperbola.

subject: Chemistry
q:
Given below are two statements :
Statement I: When an electric discharge is passed through gaseous hydrogen, the hydrogen molecules dissociate and the energetically excited hydrogen atoms produce electromagnetic radiation of discrete frequencies.
Statement II: The frequency of second line of Balmer series obtained from $\mathrm{He}^{+}$is equal to that of first line of Lyman series obtained from hydrogen atom.
In the light of the above statements, choose the correct answer from the options given below :
question:
Given below are two statements :
Statement I: When an electric discharge is passed through gaseous hydrogen, the hydrogen molecules dissociate and the energetically excited hydrogen atoms produce electromagnetic radiation of discrete frequencies.
Statement II: The frequency of second line of Balmer series obtained from $\mathrm{He}^{+}$is equal to that of first line of Lyman series obtained from hydrogen atom.
In the light of the above statements, choose the correct answer from the options given below :
options: Statement I is false but Statement II is true
Both Statement I and Statement II are true
Statement I is true but Statement II is false
Both Statement I and Statement II are false
solution:
Statement I: Electric discharge through $H_2$ causes dissociation into excited H atoms which emit discrete frequencies (line spectrum). This is true.
Statement II: Using $ u = RcZ^2\left( rac{1}{n_1^2} - rac{1}{n_2^2} ight)$
For $He^+$ (Z=2), second Balmer line ($n_1=2, n_2=4$): $ u = 4Rc\left( rac{1}{4} - rac{1}{16} ight) = rac{3Rc}{4}$
For H (Z=1), first Lyman line ($n_1=1, n_2=2$): $ u = Rc\left(1 - rac{1}{4} ight) = rac{3Rc}{4}$
Both frequencies are equal. Statement II is true.
Both statements are true.

subject: Chemistry
q:
The electron in the nth  orbit of Li2+  is excited to (n+1) orbit using the radiation of energy 1.47×10-17 J (as shown in the diagram). The value of n is ___________

Given : RH=2.18×10-18 J
question:
The electron in the nth  orbit of Li2+  is excited to (n+1) orbit using the radiation of energy 1.47×10-17 J (as shown in the diagram). The value of n is ___________

Given : RH=2.18×10-18 J
solution:
The difference energy between two energy levels is 

∆E=2.18×10-18 1n12-1n22 J

1.47×10-17=2.18×10-18×(9)1(n)2-1(n+1)2

0.749=1n2-1(n+1)2

n = 1

Hence, the transition is taking place between 1→2, and it comes under Lymann series.

If we take 0.749≃0.75=34

subject: Chemistry
q:
Following figure shows spectrum of an ideal black body at four different temperatures. The number of correct statement/s from the following is _______.

A. T4 > T3 > T2 > T1
 B. The black body consists of particles performing simple harmonic motion.
 C. The peak of the spectrum shifts to shorter wavelength as temperature increases.
 D. T1ν1 = T2ν2 = T3ν3
 E. The given spectrum could be explained using quantisation of energy
question:
Following figure shows spectrum of an ideal black body at four different temperatures. The number of correct statement/s from the following is _______.

A. T4 > T3 > T2 > T1
 B. The black body consists of particles performing simple harmonic motion.
 C. The peak of the spectrum shifts to shorter wavelength as temperature increases.
 D. T1ν1 = T2ν2 = T3ν3
 E. The given spectrum could be explained using quantisation of energy
solution: The spectrum of Black body radiation is explained using quantization of energy. With increase in temperature, peak of spectrum shifts to shorter wavelength or higher frequency. Thus, for above graph T1 > T2 > T3 > T4

subject: Chemistry
q:
If the work function of a metal is 6.63×10-19 J, the maximum wavelength of the photon required to remove a photoelectron from the metal is____nm. Nearest integer)
[Given : h=6.63×10-34 J s, and c=3×108 m s-1]
question:
If the work function of a metal is 6.63×10-19 J, the maximum wavelength of the photon required to remove a photoelectron from the metal is____nm. Nearest integer)
[Given : h=6.63×10-34 J s, and c=3×108 m s-1]
solution:
Eincident=W0+K.E.

As K.E is zero

Eincident=W0

6.63×10-19=hcλ=6.63×10-34×3×108λ

λ=3×10-7 m=300 nm.

subject: Chemistry
q: The number of radial and angular nodes in <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>4</mn><mo> </mo><mi mathvariant="normal">d</mi></math> orbital are, respectively
question: The number of radial and angular nodes in <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>4</mn><mo> </mo><mi mathvariant="normal">d</mi></math> orbital are, respectively
solution:
Number of Radial nodes =n-l-1

Number of angular nodes =l

Where,

l=Azimuthal quantum number n=principal quantum number 

For 4d orbital, n=4 and l=2

In 4d orbital the number of Radial nodes=4-2-1=1

In 4d orbital the number of Angular nodes =l=2.

Thus, the number of radial and angular nodes in 4d orbital are 1 and 2 respectively.

subject: Chemistry
q:
The minimum energy that must be possessed by photons in order to produce the photoelectric effect with platinum metal is:

[Given: The threshold frequency of platinum is 1.3×1015 s-1 and h=6.6×10-34Js]
question:
The minimum energy that must be possessed by photons in order to produce the photoelectric effect with platinum metal is:

[Given: The threshold frequency of platinum is 1.3×1015 s-1 and h=6.6×10-34Js]
solution:
Radiation energy = threshold energy of metal + kinetic energy of ejected electron

For minimum energy, 

Radiation energy = threshold energy of metal

E=hνE=6.6×10-34×1.3×1015=8.58×10-19J

subject: Chemistry
q:
Arrange the following atomic orbitals of multi electron atoms in order of increasing energy.
A. $n = 3, l = 2, m = +1$
B. $n = 4, l = 0, m = 0$
C. $n = 6, l = 1, m = 0$
D. $n = 5, l = 1, m = +1$
E. $n = 2, l = 1, m = +1$
Choose the correct answer from the options given below:
question:
Arrange the following atomic orbitals of multi electron atoms in order of increasing energy.
A. $n = 3, l = 2, m = +1$
B. $n = 4, l = 0, m = 0$
C. $n = 6, l = 1, m = 0$
D. $n = 5, l = 1, m = +1$
E. $n = 2, l = 1, m = +1$
Choose the correct answer from the options given below:
solution:
The energy of an atomic orbital in a multi-electron atom is determined by the $(n + l)$ rule. Orbitals with a lower value of $(n + l)$ have lower energy. If two orbitals have the same $(n + l)$ value, the one with the lower $n$ value has lower energy.

Calculating the $(n + l)$ value for each given orbital:
A. $n = 3, l = 2 \Rightarrow n + l = 5$
B. $n = 4, l = 0 \Rightarrow n + l = 4$
C. $n = 6, l = 1 \Rightarrow n + l = 7$
D. $n = 5, l = 1 \Rightarrow n + l = 6$
E. $n = 2, l = 1 \Rightarrow n + l = 3$

Arranging the orbitals in increasing order of their $(n + l)$ values, we get:
$3 < 4 < 5 < 6 < 7$

This corresponds to the order:
$E < B < A < D < C$

Answer: $E < B < A < D < C$

subject: Chemistry
q:
The wave function Ψ of 2 s is given by

Ψ2s=122π1a01/22-ra0e-r/2a0

At r=r0, radial node is formed. Thus, r0 in terms of a0
question:
The wave function Ψ of 2 s is given by

Ψ2s=122π1a01/22-ra0e-r/2a0

At r=r0, radial node is formed. Thus, r0 in terms of a0
solution:
The wave function (Ψ) of 2s is given by,

Ψ2s=122π1a01/22-ra0e-r/2a0

At r = r0, radial node is formed.

Ψ2s2 = Probability density of finding an electron

At node, Ψ2s2 = 0 (∵Probability of finding an electron is zero at the node)

Ψ2s2 =122π1a01/22-ra0e-r/2a02

⇒ 0 =18π1a0 2-r0a02e-2r0/a0

⇒ 2 - r0a0 = 0

⇒ 2 = r0a0

⇒ 2a0 = r0

So, the correct option is D.

subject: Chemistry
q:
When light of wavelength 248 nm falls on a metal of threshold energy 3.0 eV, the de-Broglie wavelength of emitted electrons is ________ Ao. (Round off to the Nearest Integer).

[Use : 3=1.73, h=6.63×10-34 Js; me=9.1×10-31 kg; c=3.0×108 ms-1; 1 eV=1.6×10-19 J]
question:
When light of wavelength 248 nm falls on a metal of threshold energy 3.0 eV, the de-Broglie wavelength of emitted electrons is ________ Ao. (Round off to the Nearest Integer).

[Use : 3=1.73, h=6.63×10-34 Js; me=9.1×10-31 kg; c=3.0×108 ms-1; 1 eV=1.6×10-19 J]
solution:
Energy incident =hcλ

=6.63×10-34×3.0×108248×10-9×1.6×10-19 eV

=6.63×3×100248×1.6

=0.05 eV×100=5 eV

Now using

E=ϕ+K.E

5=3+K.E.

K.E.=2eV=3.2×10-19 J

for de Broglie wavelength λ=hmv

K.E.=12mv2

so v=2K.E.m

hence λ=h2K.E.×m

=6.63×10-342×3.2×10-19×9.1×10-31

=6.637.6×10-3410-25=66.3×10-10 m7.6

=8.72×10-10 m

≈9×10-10 m

=9 Ao

subject: Chemistry
q:
According to Bohr's atomic theory:
(A) Kinetic energy of electron is ∝Z2n2.
(B) The product of velocity (v) of electron and principal quantum number n, 'vn'∝Z2.
(C) Frequency of revolution of electron in an orbit is ∝Z3n3.
(D) Coulombic force of attraction on the electron is ∝Z3n4.

Choose the most appropriate answer from the options given below:
question:
According to Bohr's atomic theory:
(A) Kinetic energy of electron is ∝Z2n2.
(B) The product of velocity (v) of electron and principal quantum number n, 'vn'∝Z2.
(C) Frequency of revolution of electron in an orbit is ∝Z3n3.
(D) Coulombic force of attraction on the electron is ∝Z3n4.

Choose the most appropriate answer from the options given below:
solution:
According to the Bohr's atomic theory, the kinetic energy of an electron is directly proportional to the square of the atomic number and inversely proportional to the square of the principal quantum number. 

(A) KE=13.6z2n2eVatom⇒KEαz2n2

(B) speed of e-αzn

∴  v×nαz

(C) Frequency of revolution of e-=v2πr

∴ Frequency αz2n3

(D) Coulombic force of attraction on the electron is directly proportional to the square of the atomic number and inversely proportional to the fourth power of the principal quantum number. 

 F=kq1q2r2=kze2r2  rαn2z

⇒ Fαzn2z2

⇒  Fαz3n4

q:
An accelerated electron has a speed of 5×106 ms-1 with an uncertainty of 0.02%. The uncertainty in finding its location while in motion is x×10-9 m.

[Use mass of electron=9.1×10-31 kg,

h=6.63×10-34Js,π=3.14
question:
An accelerated electron has a speed of 5×106 ms-1 with an uncertainty of 0.02%. The uncertainty in finding its location while in motion is x×10-9 m.
The value of x is __________. (Nearest integer)

[Use mass of electron=9.1×10-31 kg,

h=6.63×10-34Js,π=3.14
solution:
Δv=0.02100×5×106=103 m s-1

Δx·Δv=h4πm

x×10-9×103=6.63×10-344×3.14×9.1×10-31

x×10-9×103=0.058×10-3

x=0.058×10-610-9=58

question: The energy of one mole of photons of radiation of wavelength <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>300</mn><mo> </mo><mi>nm</mi></math> is (Given : <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi mathvariant="normal">h</mi><mo>=</mo><mn>6</mn><mo>.</mo><mn>63</mn><mo>×</mo><msup><mn>10</mn><mrow><mo>-</mo><mn>34</mn></mrow></msup><mo> </mo><mi mathvariant="normal">J</mi><mo> </mo><mi mathvariant="normal">s</mi><mo>,</mo><msub><mi mathvariant="normal">N</mi><mi mathvariant="normal">A</mi></msub><mo>=</mo><mn>6</mn><mo>.</mo><mn>02</mn><mo>×</mo><msup><mn>10</mn><mn>23</mn></msup><msup><mrow><mo> </mo><mi>mol</mi></mrow><mrow><mo>-</mo><mn>1</mn></mrow></msup><mi mathvariant="normal">c</mi><mo>=</mo><mn>3</mn><mo>×</mo><msup><mn>10</mn><mn>8</mn></msup><mo> </mo><mi mathvariant="normal">m</mi><msup><mrow><mo> </mo><mi mathvariant="normal">s</mi></mrow><mrow><mo>-</mo><mn>1</mn></mrow></msup></math>)
solution:
λ=300×10−9m

Energy of single photon =hcλ

Energy of NA photons =6.02×1023×6.63×10−34×3×108300×10−9

= 3.99×105 J/mole=399 KJ/mole

subject: Chemistry
q: Which transition in the hydrogen spectrum would have the same wavelength as the Balmer type transition from <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi mathvariant="normal">n</mi><mo>=</mo><mn>4</mn></math> to <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi mathvariant="normal">n</mi><mo>=</mo><mn>2</mn></math> of <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><msup><mi>He</mi><mo>+</mo></msup></math>spectrum
question: Which transition in the hydrogen spectrum would have the same wavelength as the Balmer type transition from <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi mathvariant="normal">n</mi><mo>=</mo><mn>4</mn></math> to <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi mathvariant="normal">n</mi><mo>=</mo><mn>2</mn></math> of <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><msup><mi>He</mi><mo>+</mo></msup></math>spectrum
solution:
For He+ ion, the wave number associated with the Balmer transition, n = 4 to n = 2 is given by:

1λ = RZ2 1n12 - 1n22

Where, n1 = 2

n2 = 4

Z = atomic number of helium

1λ=R(2)2122-142

⇒1λ=4R4-116

⇒1λ= 3R4

⇒λ = 43R

According to the question, the desired transition for hydrogen will have the same wavelength as that of He+.

λ(H)=λHe+

R(1)21n12-1n22 = 3R4

By hit and trial method, 

1n12-1n22=112-122

On comparing n1=1 & n2=2

So, the correct answer is n = 2 to n = 1.

subject: Chemistry
q:
Given below are two statements :
Statement (\(\mathbf{I}\)): The radii of isoelectronic species increases in the order.
\(\mathrm{Mg}^{2+} \lt \mathrm{Na}^{+} \lt \mathrm{F}^{-} \lt \mathrm{O}^{2-}\)
Statement (II): The magnitude of electron gain enthalpy of halogen decreases in the order.
\(\mathrm{Cl}\gt\mathrm{F}\gt\mathrm{Br}\gt\mathrm{I}\)
In the light of the above statements, choose the most appropriate answer from the options given below :
question:
Given below are two statements :
Statement (\(\mathbf{I}\)): The radii of isoelectronic species increases in the order.
\(\mathrm{Mg}^{2+} \lt \mathrm{Na}^{+} \lt \mathrm{F}^{-} \lt \mathrm{O}^{2-}\)
Statement (II): The magnitude of electron gain enthalpy of halogen decreases in the order.
\(\mathrm{Cl}\gt\mathrm{F}\gt\mathrm{Br}\gt\mathrm{I}\)
In the light of the above statements, choose the most appropriate answer from the options given below :
options: Statement I is incorrect but Statement II is correct
Statement I is correct but Statement II is incorrect
Both Statement I and Statement II are incorrect
Both Statement I and Statement II are correct
solution:
$\mathrm{r} \propto \mathrm{q}^{-}$(for isoelectronic species)
$\propto \frac{1}{q^{+}}$
$\therefore$ Statement I is correct
Magnitude of electron gain enthalpy
$\mathrm{Cl}>\mathrm{F}>\mathrm{Br}>\mathrm{I}$

subject: Chemistry
q:
Heat treatment of muscular pain involves radiation of wavelength of about 900 nm . Which spectral line of H atom is suitable for this?
Given : Rydberg constant $\mathrm{R}_{\mathrm{H}}=10^5 \mathrm{~cm}^{-1}, \mathrm{~h}=6.6 \times 10^{-34} \mathrm{~J} \mathrm{~s}, \mathrm{c}=3 \times 10^8 \mathrm{~m} / \mathrm{s}$ )
question:
Heat treatment of muscular pain involves radiation of wavelength of about 900 nm . Which spectral line of H atom is suitable for this?
Given : Rydberg constant $\mathrm{R}_{\mathrm{H}}=10^5 \mathrm{~cm}^{-1}, \mathrm{~h}=6.6 \times 10^{-34} \mathrm{~J} \mathrm{~s}, \mathrm{c}=3 \times 10^8 \mathrm{~m} / \mathrm{s}$ )
solution:
$\begin{aligned}
& \lambda=900 \mathrm{~nm} \quad \text { H-atom }(\mathrm{Z}=1) \\
& =9 \times 10^{-5} \mathrm{~cm} \\
& \mathrm{R}_{\mathrm{H}}=10^5 \mathrm{~cm}^{-1} \\
& \text { Ryderg eq. }=\frac{1}{\lambda}=\mathrm{R}_{\mathrm{H}} \mathrm{Z}^2 \times\left(\frac{1}{\mathrm{n}_1^2}-\frac{1}{\mathrm{n}_2^2}\right) \\
& \Rightarrow \frac{1}{\lambda \times \mathrm{R}_{\mathrm{H}}}=\frac{1}{\mathrm{n}_1^2}-\frac{1}{\mathrm{n}_2^2} \\
& \Rightarrow \frac{1}{9 \times 10^{-5} \mathrm{~cm} \times 10^5 \mathrm{~cm}^{-1}}=\left(\frac{1}{\mathrm{n}_1^2}-\frac{1}{\mathrm{n}_2^2}\right) \\
& \Rightarrow \frac{1}{\mathrm{n}_1^2}-\frac{1}{\mathrm{n}_2^2}=\frac{1}{9}
\end{aligned}$

It is possible when $\mathrm{n}_1=3, \mathrm{n}_2=\infty$
Possible series : $\infty \rightarrow 3$

subject: Chemistry
q: The number of orbitals associated with quantum numbers <math><mi mathvariant="normal">n</mi><mo>=</mo><mn>5</mn><mo>,</mo><mi mathvariant="normal"></mi><msub><mrow><mi mathvariant="normal">m</mi></mrow><mrow><mi mathvariant="normal">s</mi></mrow></msub><mo>=</mo><mo>+</mo><mfrac><mrow><mn>1</mn></mrow><mrow><mn>2</mn></mrow></mfrac></math> is:
question: The number of orbitals associated with quantum numbers <math><mi mathvariant="normal">n</mi><mo>=</mo><mn>5</mn><mo>,</mo><mi mathvariant="normal"></mi><msub><mrow><mi mathvariant="normal">m</mi></mrow><mrow><mi mathvariant="normal">s</mi></mrow></msub><mo>=</mo><mo>+</mo><mfrac><mrow><mn>1</mn></mrow><mrow><mn>2</mn></mrow></mfrac></math> is:
solution:
For n=5   5s, 5p, 5d, 5f  and 5g and subshell is available

In   5s→1 orbital

5p→3 orbital

5d→5 orbital

5f→7 orbital

5g→9 orbital

In each subshell 1 electron of every orbital will belong to m 5 =+ 1 2 So, Total number of orbitals that will be associate with  m 5 =+ 1 2   will be 1+3+5+7+9=25

solution: $\begin{aligned} & \lambda=\frac{\mathrm{h}}{\mathrm{mv}} \\ & \lambda=\frac{\mathrm{hv}}{\mathrm{mv}^2} \\ & \frac{\mathrm{mv}^2}{\mathrm{~h}}=\frac{\mathrm{v}}{\lambda}=\mathrm{v} \text { (frequency) } \\ & \text { Given } \frac{1}{2} \mathrm{mv}^2=2.18 \times 10^{-18} \mathrm{~J} \\ & \mathrm{~h}=6.6 \times 10^{-34} \\ & \mathrm{v}=\frac{4.36 \times 10^{-18}}{6.6 \times 10^{-34}}=660.60 \times 10^{13} \mathrm{~Hz} \\ & \approx 661 \times 10^{13} \mathrm{~Hz}\end{aligned}$

subject: Chemistry
q: The energy of an electron in the first Bohr orbit of hydrogen atom is -2.18×10-18 J Its energy in the third Bohr orbit is_______.
question: The energy of an electron in the first Bohr orbit of hydrogen atom is -2.18×10-18 J Its energy in the third Bohr orbit is_______.
solution:
The energy of an electron in the nth Bohr orbit of a hydrogen atom can be calculated using the formula:

En=-2.18×10-18Z2n2Where n is the principal quantum number of the Bohr orbit.Enα1n2Enα19

Therefore, the energy of the electron in the third Bohr orbit of a hydrogen atom is Three times of this value.

subject: Chemistry
q: According to Bohr's model of hydrogen atom, which of the following statement is incorrect?
question: According to Bohr's model of hydrogen atom, which of the following statement is incorrect?
options: Radius of \(3^{\text {rd }}\) orbit is nine times larger than that of \(1^{\text {st }}\) orbit.
Radius of \(8^{\text {th }}\) orbit is four times larger than that of \(4^{\text {th }}\) orbit.
Radius of \(6^{\text {th }}\) orbit is three time larger than that of \(4^{\text {th }}\) orbit.
Radius of \(4^{\text {th }}\) orbit is four times larger than that of \(2^{\text {nd }}\) orbit.
solution:
\(\mathrm{r} \propto \mathrm{n}^2\)
(1) \(\frac{\mathrm{r}_3}{\mathrm{r}_1}=\frac{9}{1}\)
(2) \(\frac{\mathrm{r}_8}{\mathrm{r}_4}=\frac{64}{16}=4\)
(3) \(\frac{\mathrm{r}_6}{\mathrm{r}_4}=\left(\frac{6}{4}\right)^2=\frac{9}{4}\)
(4) \(\frac{\mathrm{r}_4}{\mathrm{r}_2}=\left(\frac{4}{2}\right)^2=4\)
